coredata.dc:title: Design, synthesis and biological evaluation of novel dual-targeting fluorescent probes for detection of Fe3+ in the lysosomes of hepatocytes mediated by galactose-morpholine moieties
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yan
Liu, Feiyang
Yi, Qingyuan
Wang, Mian
Wang, Jianyi
coredata.dc:description: In this work, novel dual-targeting probes composed of galactose and morpholine were designed and synthesized for monitoring Fe3+ levels in the lysosome of hepatocyte. MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 showed good selectivity and sensitivities toward Fe3+ with the detection limits of 9.40 × 10−8 M, 7.68 × 10−8 M and 7.10 × 10−8 M, respectively. 1:2 stoichiometry is the most likely recognition mode between probe and Fe3+. Low toxic MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 exhibited favorable hepatic targeting effect in both cell and tissue levels, which was because the galactose group of probe could be recognized by ASGPR overexpressed on the hepatocytes. The hepatocyte-targeting capacity followed MP-Gal-1 < MP-Gal-2 < MP-Gal-3 trend, which was attributed to the galactose cluster effect. MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 also displayed good lysosomes-targeting capacities, because the basic morpholine moiety of probes could be easily attracted by the acidic lysosome. Therefore, MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 have good dual targeting capacities (liver and lysosome) and could be used to detect lysosomal Fe3+ in the liver, which is great significant for precise diagnosis and treatment of liver lysosomal iron-related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
Galactose
Morpholine
Fe3+
Hepatocyte-targeting
Lysosomal-targeting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity assays of the probes toward Fe3+
The response assays of probes to Fe3+ in hepatoma cells and in vitro hepatic targeting assays of probes
The in vivo hepatic targeting assays of probes and their response on Fe3+ in liver tissues
Selectivity and sensitivity of the aimed probes toward Fe3+ in the solution
Stoichiometry and recognition mode of the aimed probes toward Fe3+
Response of the aimed probes toward Fe3+ at the cellular and tissue levels
Hepatocyte-targeting capacities of the aimed probes
Lysosomes-targeting capacities of the aimed probes
Comparison of the aimed probes with the reported Fe3+ fluorescent sensors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81960649 , 21967003 ), Guangxi Natural Science Foundation ( 2018JJA140372 ), the Scientific Research Staring Foundation for Young Doctor of Guangxi University ( XBZ190880 ), the Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology (No. 2019K007 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structures of the designed probes.

Fluorescence responses of MP-Gal-1 (A), MP-Gal-2 (B) and MP-Gal-3 (C) in deionized water upon addition of 10 equiv metal ion (probe concentration = 2 μM, DMSO content = 0.002%, λex = 405 nm, λem = 450–800 nm, slit widths = 5 nm/5 nm).

Fluorescence intensity changes of MP-Gal-1(A), MP-Gal-2(B), and MP-Gal-3(C) in deionized water (probe concentration: 2 μM, DMSO content: 0.002%) with the addition of Fe3+ solution (0–10 equiv., that is 0–20 μM) and the correlation curve between maximum fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration.
Job's plot of the representative probe MP-Gal-1 toward Fe3+ in deionized water (DMSO content <0.01%).

(A) HepG2 cells incubated with dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) containing 10 μM of MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 (DMSO content: 0.01%) for 20 min, and then incubated with deionized water containing 0 μM or 25 μM of Fe3+ for 10 min at 37 °C, respectively. (B) The corresponding fluorescence intensity calculated by Image J (n = 3, **P < 0.01).

(A) Fluorescence distribution of MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 in mouse liver tissue treated with or without Fe3+. The doses are 5.6 mg/kg for MP-Gal-1, 8.1 mg/kg for MP-Gal-2, and 10.3 mg/kg for MP-Gal-3, probes are dissolved in mixed solvent comprised of saline and DMSO, and the DMSO contents are less than 0.1%. (B) Fluorescence quantification processed by Image J (n = 3, **P < 0.01).
Cell viability of HepG2 incubated with DMEM containing different concentrations of MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 (DMSO content <0.1%).

(A) In vitro hepatocyte-targeting capacities of the aimed probes (scale bar, 50 μm). HepG2, Hela, A549 and SGC-7901 cells were incubated with DMEM containing 10 μM of probes (DMSO content: 0.01%) for 20 min, and then incubated with Fe3+ solution (25 μM) at 37 °C for 10 min. (B) The fluorescence intensity of cells calculated by Image J (n = 3, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01).

(A) In vivo hepatocyte-targeting capacities of the aimed probes (scale bar, 50 μm). At 3 h post-injection of saline, 5.6 mg/kg of MP-Gal-1, 8.1 mg/kg of MP-Gal-2 and 10.3 mg/kg of MP-Gal-3 (the mixed solvent of probes: saline and DMSO, DMSO content <0.1%), respectively, the tissue sections of mice were harvested, processed and treated with 50 μM of Fe3+. (B) Fluorescence quantification in different tissue sections (n = 3, **P < 0.01, ns = not significant).

Lysosomal targeting images of probes in HepG2 cells (scale bar: 25 μm). (A) The green fluorescence of MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 (10 μM of probes dissolved in DMEM with DMSO content of 0.01%). (B) The red fluorescence of commercial lysosomal probes (Lyso-Tracker Red, LT). It should be noted that 50 nM of Lyso-Tracker Red was dissolved in DMEM with DMSO content of 0.005%. (C) The overlap images of probes and Lyso-Tracker Red. (D) The corresponding fluorescence profile analysis. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Synthetic route of the aimed probe MP-Gal-3.
Comparison of proposed probes with the reported Fe3+ fluorescent sensors.

row.entry: Lysosome of liver cell or tissue
Lysosome of liver cell or tissue
Lysosome of liver cell or tissue

head.ce:title: Design, synthesis and biological evaluation of novel dual-targeting fluorescent probes for detection of Fe3+ in the lysosomes of hepatocytes mediated by galactose-morpholine moieties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science and Technology, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science and Technology, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medical College, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Medical College, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Y. W. and F.Y. L. contributed equally to this work as first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, novel dual-targeting probes composed of galactose and morpholine were designed and synthesized for monitoring Fe3+ levels in the lysosome of hepatocyte. MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 showed good selectivity and sensitivities toward Fe3+ with the detection limits of 9.40 × 10−8 M, 7.68 × 10−8 M and 7.10 × 10−8 M, respectively. 1:2 stoichiometry is the most likely recognition mode between probe and Fe3+. Low toxic MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 exhibited favorable hepatic targeting effect in both cell and tissue levels, which was because the galactose group of probe could be recognized by ASGPR overexpressed on the hepatocytes. The hepatocyte-targeting capacity followed MP-Gal-1 < MP-Gal-2 < MP-Gal-3 trend, which was attributed to the galactose cluster effect. MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 also displayed good lysosomes-targeting capacities, because the basic morpholine moiety of probes could be easily attracted by the acidic lysosome. Therefore, MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 have good dual targeting capacities (liver and lysosome) and could be used to detect lysosomal Fe3+ in the liver, which is great significant for precise diagnosis and treatment of liver lysosomal iron-related diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is an important trace element in the body, which involved in biological processes such as oxygen transport, electron transport and oxidative metabolism [1,2]. Liver is the main iron storage organ [3,4], and the iron concentration (especially Fe3+ ion) in the liver is an indicator for the total amount of iron in the body [5]. Furthermore, liver secretes a circulating peptide hormone, ferritin, which plays a key role in the regulation of iron homeostasis. Therefore, the determination of Fe3+ concentration in the liver is very significant for monitoring iron-overload diseases involved in sickle cell anemia, myelodysplasia, thalassemia, aplastic anemia and neurodegenerative diseases [6–9], as well as iron deficiency diseases involved in anemia, hemochromatosis and liver damage [10,11]. On the other hand, abnormal iron levels in the lysosome would cause lysosomal internal instability, aberrant lysosomal membrane permeability and acid microenvironment, leading to acquired lysosomal storage diseases [12]. Clearly, monitoring Fe3+ concentration level in the liver lysosome is very significant and an indicator for precise diagnosis of liver lysosomal iron-related diseases.

Voltammetry, plasma mass spectrometry, atomic absorption spectrometry and colorimetry are common methods to determine Fe3+ concentration [13]. However, complicated pretreatment of sample, time-consuming, low resolution, high cost and poor bio-compatibility limit the wide application of these methods in biological system. The fluorescent-probe method has recently received extensive attentions from academia and industry because of its high efficiency and selection, economy and convenient manipulation. A rhodamine B conjugate was reported to have good selectivity for Fe3+, superior cell permeability and low toxicity at 10 μM, and its detection limit was 3.96 × 10−7 M [14]. A fluorescent probe based on isocoumarin APICP could be used to detect trace amounts of Fe3+ in HepG2 cells and had 50% of cell survival rate at 0.541 mM [15]. The Nap-Glc fluorescent probe could detect Fe3+ in HepG2 cells with good water solubility and cell permeability [16]. However, high detection limits, low selectivity, long response time, poor biocompatibility and especially poor targeting-capacity limit the wide application of these probes in the biosystems. Furthermore, few probes are reported to precisely monitor Fe3+ in the lysosomes of hepatocyte up to date.

It is well known that galactose can be recognized by asialoglycoprotein receptor (ASGPR) overexpressed on the hepatocytes with a cluster effect [17]. Lysosome is an acidic organelle and have a certain affinity for basic groups such as morpholine [18–21]. However, the galactose derivatives can only target hepatocytes but cannot specifically target lysosomes, while the morpholine derivatives can selectively recognize lysosomes but cannot specifically recognize hepatocytes.

To precisely monitor Fe3+ in the liver lysosome, in this work, novel dual-targeting fluorescent probes were designed for detection of Fe3+ in the lysosomes of hepatocytes with galactose as liver-targeting group, morpholine as lysosomal-targeting group and naphthalimide as fluorescence response group. To fully take advantage of galactose-recognition cluster effect toward ASGPR, the probe was successfully modified by one, two and three galactoses to afford mono-, bi- and tridentate galactose probes (MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2, and MP-Gal-3), respectively (Fig. 1 ). These dual targeting probes are subsequently synthesized (Scheme 1), and in vivo and in vitro experiments were conducted to explore their application in detection of Fe3+ in the biosystem.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity assays of the probes toward Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The probe solution (2 μM) was added to different ions solutions and different concentrations of Fe3+ solutions (0–10 equiv.). The fluorescence intensity was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer at the excitation wavelength of 405 nm (scanning range 450–800 nm) with slit widths of 5 nm/5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response assays of probes to Fe3+ in hepatoma cells and in vitro hepatic targeting assays of probes
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the response of probes to Fe3+ in hepatoma cells, HepG2 cells were incubated with 10 μM of probes (DMSO content is 0.01%) for 20 min at 37 °C and then fixed. After that, the cells were divided into two groups: the experimental group and the control group. The control group was directly treated with anti-fluorescence quenching agent. For the experimental group, cells were incubated with 25 μM of Fe3+ solution for 10 min and treated with anti-fluorescence quenching agent. Finally, cells were observed by confocal microscopy.

For in vitro hepatic targeting assays of probes, HepG2, A549, Hela and SGC-7901 cells were seeded on sterile slides and cultured for 24 h. The subsequent processes were performed using the above method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The in vivo hepatic targeting assays of probes and their response on Fe3+ in liver tissues
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the in vivo hepatic targeting assays of probes, Kunming mice were injected saline, MP-Gal-1 (5.6 mg/kg), MP-Gal-2 (8.1 mg/kg), MP-Gal-3 (10.3 mg/kg) with via tail vein, respectively. At 3 h post-injection, the tissues of mice were harvested, fixed, dehydrated, and treated with transparent agent. Next, the tissues were embedded in paraffin and sectioned into slices, which was followed by the dewaxing treatment. Subsequently, the tissue slices were treated with 50 μM Fe3+ solutions for 30 min and washed with deionized water.

To determine the response of probes on Fe3+ in liver tissues, the liver tissue slices were divided into the Fe3+ group and the without-Fe3+ group. The Fe3+ group was processed using the above method. The without-Fe3+ group was processed using the similar method but was not treated with Fe3+.
Finally, the tissue slices were treated with antifade mounting medium and then observed by confocal microscopy.

HepG2 cells were seeded in 4-chamber glass bottom dish (5 × 104 cells per chamber) and incubated for 24 h. Then, cells were incubated with 50 nM of Lyso-Tracker Red for 20 min followed by three washes with deionized water. The cells were further incubated with 10 μM of probes (DMSO content is 0.01%) for 20 min and washed with deionized water. Next, the cells were incubated with Fe3+ solutions (25 μM) for 10 min and finally observed by confocal microscopy.

As shown in Scheme 1, tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane was first protected by (Boc)2O, then its OH groups were substituted by 3-Bromo-1-propyne to give 2a. Compound 2a underwent deprotection of Boc by CF3COOH and acylation reaction to afford 3a. 3a was subsequently clicked by the previously synthesized galactose azide to afford 4a. 4a subsequently reacted with the previously synthesized morpholine naphthalimide derivatives to afford 5a, which was deprotected by NaOCH3 to afford the aimed probe MP-Gal-3. The structure of the aimed compound could be confirmed by 1H NMR, 13C NMR, HRMS. MP-Gal-1 and MP-Gal-2 could be synthesized according to similar synthetic routes (Supporting Information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of the aimed probes toward Fe3+ in the solution
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore selectivity and sensitivity of the aimed probes toward Fe
3+ in the solution, the fluorescence spectra of MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 in deionized water were investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 , the fluorescence at 530 nm of MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 solution was significantly enhanced upon the addition of Fe3+ to the probe solutions, while no obvious fluorescence enhancements were observed upon addition of Co2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, K+, NH4 +, Ca2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Na+, Ce3+, Al3+, Zr4+ and Cs2+ ions. As we know, naphthalimide derivatives are a class of classic fluorescent probes with photoinduced electron transfer (PET) effect. The electron-donating effect of the nitrogen atom at the end of piperazine in the probe quenches the fluorescence of the naphthalimide. However, when the nitrogen atom is selectively bound by Fe3+ ions instead of other metal ions, the electron donating effect of the nitrogen atom is inhibited, and thus the PET effect in the naphthalimide is blocked, resulting in the emission of strong fluorescence. To elucidate whether the acidic environment of lysosome affects the detection of Fe
3+ by the probe, the response experiment of the probes under different pH conditions in vitro were performed. As shown in Fig. S1, in the range of pH 4–7 (especially in the acidic range of lysosome, pH = 4–5.5), the fluorescence intensity of probes treated with Fe3+ was significantly higher than that of probes treated without Fe3+, indicating that the aimed probes possess good response capacities toward Fe3+ in acidic conditions of lysosome. In other words, detection of lysosomal Fe
3+ by the probes was not disturbed by the acidic environment of lysosome.

From the fluorescence titration experiments in Fig. 3 , the MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 solutions exhibited very weak fluorescence at 530 nm without Fe3+ but showed increasing fluorescence with the addition of Fe3+, which was also attributed to the binding of Fe3+ to the nitrogen atom of piperazine ring, inhibiting PET effect of naphthalimide in the probes. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 solutions toward Fe3+ showed a linear correlation with R1 2 = 0.955, R2 2 = 0.969 and R3 2 = 0.967, respectively. The detection limit toward Fe
3+ was calculated to be 9.40 × 10−8 M for MP-Gal-1, 7.68 × 10−8 M for MP-Gal-2 and 7.10 × 10−8 M for MP-Gal-3, separately. Therefore, MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 have good selectivities and sensitivities toward Fe3+, which could be used to qualitatively and quantitatively detect Fe3+ in the solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stoichiometry and recognition mode of the aimed probes toward Fe3+
Response of the aimed probes toward Fe3+ at the cellular and tissue levels
ce:section.ce:para: To expand the application of the aimed probes in biological systems, the response experiment of MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 toward Fe3+ in the cellular and tissue environments were performed. As shown in Fig. 5 , the HepG2 cells incubated with MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 did not display obvious fluorescence when Fe3+ was not added, while the fluorescence intensities were enhanced 1.903, 1.431 and 3.891 times when Fe3+ was added, respectively. These data indicated that the aimed probes have good responses to Fe3+ in cell environment. Seen from Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity of liver tissue treated with 50 μΜ of Fe3+ was 1.770, 1.733 and 1.384 times higher than that of liver tissue treated without Fe3+, respectively, showing that the aimed probes possess good response capacities toward Fe3+ in liver tissue.

To determine the safety of the aimed probes in the biological system, the cytotoxicity experiments were carried out. As shown in Fig. 7 , as the concentration of MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 increased, the cell viability slowly decreased. When the concentration of MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 reached 320 μM, the cell viability was still more than 78%. Clearly, the aimed probes presented low cytotoxicity and could be safely used to detect Fe3+ in the biological system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hepatocyte-targeting capacities of the aimed probes
ce:section.ce:para: To explore whether the aimed probes can selectively distinguish liver cells from other tissue cells, cell imaging experiments of MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 in HepG2, Hela, A549 and SGC7901 cells were performed. Seen from Fig. 8 , the fluorescence intensity of MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 in the HepG2 (hepatoma cells) was significantly higher than that in Hela (cervical carcinoma cells), A549 (lung cancer cells) and SGC7901 (gastric cancer cells), showing good hepatocyte-targeting capacities of these probes. This is attributed to a fact that the galactose group of probes can be recognized by ASGPR overexpressed on the hepatocytes and hardly recognized by Hela, A549 and SGC7901 cell lacking of ASGPR. Furthermore, the overall trend followed MP-Gal-1 (monodentate-galactose) < MP-Gal-2 (bidentate-galactose) < MP-Gal-3 (tridentate-galactose). This could be due to a galactose-recognition cluster effect toward ASGPR (the more the number of modified galactose groups, the more significant the hepatocyte-targeting effect). Obviously, MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 can selectively distinguish liver cells from other tissue cells.

To further evaluate the in vivo hepatocyte-targeting capacity of the aimed probes, the distributions of MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 in various tissues of mice were compared. As shown in Fig. 9 , the fluorescence intensity of MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 in the liver were significantly higher than that in the heart, spleen, lung and kidney, whereas the negative group (saline group) did not show clear fluorescence in all tissues. Clearly, MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 can accumulate in the liver tissue rather than heart, spleen, lung and kidney. When the fluorophore was modified by two galactose ligands, the obtained bidentate-galactose MP-Gal-2 showed higher accumulated amount in the liver tissue than MP-Gal-1 with one galactose ligand. When the fluorophore was modified by three galactose ligands, the obtained tridentate-galactose MP-Gal-3 achieves highest accumulated amount in the liver tissue among these probes. In other words, the in vivo hepatocyte-targeting capacity followed MP-Gal-1 < MP-Gal-2 < MP-Gal-3 trend, being consistent with the in vitro experiments. Therefore, the aimed probes could accumulate in the liver tissue mediated by ASGPR overexpressed on hepatocyte membrane (excellent in vivo hepatocyte-targeting capacity) and selectively recognize Fe3+ in the liver tissues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lysosomes-targeting capacities of the aimed probes
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate whether the aimed probes accurately located in lysosome and detected lysosomal Fe3+ in living cells, HepG2 cells were successively incubated with commercial lysosome probe (Lyso-Tracker Red), the aimed probes (MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3) and 25 μM Fe3+ solution, respectively. As shown in Fig. 10 , MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 showed green fluorescence (Fig. 10A) and Lyso-Tracker Red showed red fluorescence (Fig. 10B), respectively. Moreover, the aimed probes (MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3) and Lyso-Tracker Red were well merged in HepG2 cells (Fig. 10C–D) with the pearson overlap coefficients of 0.830, 0.841, and 0.866, respectively. This indicated that MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 had good lysosome-locating capacities and could be used to detect lysosomal Fe3+ in liver cells. The reason is that MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 contain basic morpholine moiety, which are easy to be attracted by acidic environment of the lysosome.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the aimed probes with the reported Fe3+ fluorescent sensors
ce:section.ce:para: A comparison of the aimed probes with the reported Fe3+ fluorescent sensors are summarized in Table 1 . It should be noted that MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 exhibited high selectivity toward Fe3+ and was not disturbed by Co2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, K+, NH4 +, Ca2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Na+, Ce3+, Al3+, Zr4+ and Cs2+, while other reported Fe3+ sensors were usually disturbed by some metal cations such as Cu2+ and Hg2+. MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 showed an excellent detection limit (9.40 × 10−8 M, 7.68 × 10−8 M and 7.10 × 10−8 M) compared with many reported sensors [22,25,36]. Interestingly, MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 showed less toxicity in higher dose range compared with the reported Fe3+ probes. More importantly, MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 not only had good hepatocyte-targeting abilities but also could accurately target lysosomes, being used to detect lysosomal Fe3+ in liver cells and tissues. However, other probes are short of the dual hepatocyte-lysosome targeting capacity.

In this work, galactose-morpholine modified dual-targeting fluorescent probes for detecting Fe3+ in the lysosomes of hepatocytes was design, synthesized and evaluated. The probes displayed superior selectivity and sensitivity toward Fe3+ with the recognition mode of 1:2 stoichiometry. The detection limits are 9.40 × 10−8 M for MP-Gal-1, 7.68 × 10−8 M for MP-Gal-2 and 7.10 × 10−8 M for MP-Gal-3, respectively. These probes with low toxicity could selectively recognize Fe3+ in the hepatocyte and liver tissues, implying the potential in biological application. MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 could selectively distinguish hepatocellular carcinoma cells from other tissue cells, and they also can significantly accumulate in liver tissue rather than heart, spleen, lung and kidney tissue, exhibiting obvious hepatocyte-targeting efficacy. This was attributed to a fact that the galactose group of probes can be recognized by ASGPR overexpressed on the hepatocytes. Moreover, the hepatocyte-targeting trend followed MP-Gal-1 (monodentate-galactose) < MP-Gal-2 (bidentate-galactose) < MP-Gal-3 (tridentate-galactose), indicating a cluster effect (the more the number of modified galactose groups, the more significant the hepatocyte targeting effect). Furthermore, MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 possessed good lysosome-targeting capacities due to the attraction between basic morpholine moiety of the probes and the acidic environment of the lysosome. Compared with other reported probes, MP-Gal-1, MP-Gal-2 and MP-Gal-3 exhibited superior hepatocyte and lysosomal dual targeting abilities and could be used to detect Fe3+ in the liver lysosome, which is very significant for precise diagnosis and treatment of hepatic lysosome iron-related diseases.

Yan Wang: Evaluation of the aimed probes. Feiyang Liu: Synthesis of the aimed probes. Qingyuan Yi: Evaluation of the aimed probes. Mian Wang: Project design, supervision, review & editing. Jianyi Wang: Project design, supervision, Writing & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81960649, 21967003), Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2018JJA140372), the Scientific Research Staring Foundation for Young Doctor of Guangxi University (XBZ190880), the Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology (No. 2019K007).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2022.123362.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemistry and biology of eukaryotic iron metabolism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Aisen, C. Enns, M. Wessling-Resnick, Chemistry and biology of eukaryotic iron metabolism, Int. J. Biochem. Cell B. 33 (2001) 940-959.
G. Papanikolaou, K. Pantopoulos, Iron metabolism and toxicity, Toxicol Appl. Pharmacol. 202 (2005) 199-211.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coincident increase in periportal expression of iron proteins in the iron-loaded rat liver

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.A. Basclain, G.P. Jeffrey, Coincident increase in periportal expression of iron proteins in the iron-loaded rat liver, J. Gastroenterol. Hepatol. 14 (1999) 659-668.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tissue iron distribution and urinary mineral excretion vary depending on the form of iron (FeSO4 or NaFeEDTA) and the route of administration (oral or subcutaneous) in rats given high doses of iron

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhu, D.D. Miller, Tissue Iron Distribution and Urinary Mineral Excretion Vary Depending on the Form of Iron (FeSO4 or NaFeEDTA) and the Route of Administration (Oral or Subcutaneous) in Rats Given High Doses of Iron, J. Agr. Food. Chem. 55 (2007) 8793-8799.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Elevated liver iron concentration is a marker of increased morbidity in patients with beta thalassemia intermedia

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.M. Musallam, M.D. Cappellini, J.C. Wood, I. Motta, G. Graziadei, H. Tamim, A.T. Taher, Elevated liver iron concentration is a marker of increased morbidity in patients with beta thalassemia intermedia, Haematologica. 96 (2011) 1605-1612.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Iron chelation therapy for patients with sickle cell disease and iron overload

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Inati, E. Khoriaty, K.M. Musallam, A.T. Taher, Iron chelation therapy for patients with sickle cell disease and iron overload, Am. J. Hematol. 85 (2010) 782-786.
